explicit_text: I am the chief of maintenance at Shenandoah National Park, and I also serve as the Operations Section Chief for the National Park Service Incident Management Team. Shenandoah is a large park east of the Mississippi and has a competent Type-3 Incident Management Team made up of park employees. During the 9/11 attacks, I was based at Shenandoah Dispatch, which is the national dispatch center, and we coordinated many of the initial responses from there.

explicit_text: I have worked extensively with the National Park Service, especially in developing and participating in their national incident management teams since about 1990. The Park Service adopted a fire model command structure for incident management, which I have been part of for over a decade. During events like Pearl Harbor commemorations and the 9/11 attacks, the National Park Service coordinated resources across many parks, including law enforcement and emergency personnel.

explicit_text: I began my involvement with incident management about 15 years ago while working special events at Independence National Historical Park. This experience led me into the incident command structure and eventually into the National Park Service's national incident management teams. Independence National Historical Park was one of the key sites we had to consider during national incidents like 9/11.

explicit_text: I have been a member of the National Park Service Incident Management Team since the early 1990s and have participated in every incident since the Pearl Harbor commemorative event in 1991. That event was one of the first major incidents where this incident management structure was tested. It helped establish the framework we use today for managing large-scale events.

explicit_text: I was in the Outer Banks of North Carolina when the 9/11 attacks happened, and I watched the second plane hit the World Trade Center while getting a haircut. I immediately contacted Shenandoah Dispatch and the incident commander to prepare for deployment. We were on the ground by 10 p.m. that Tuesday night, coordinating a national response that involved prioritizing resources to protect sites like the Statue of Liberty, Independence Hall, and Old Ironsides. The incident required 24-hour operational periods and constant revision of plans to manage the evolving situation.

entity: Office of the Secretary of the Interior
explicit_text: During the 9/11 incident, our National Park Service Incident Management Team worked closely with the Office of the Secretary of the Interior. Someone from the Secretary's office was directly involved in setting objectives and responsibilities for our team. We were tasked with coordinating resources nationally and reporting back to the Secretary's office on the status and allocation of Park Service resources.

explicit_text: Philadelphia was one of the key locations involved in the national response to 9/11. The Regional Director there called on the Type-2 Incident Management Team to coordinate efforts. Our national team worked to prioritize resources across Philadelphia, New York City, Boston, and other sites to ensure effective protection and response.

Our national team worked to prioritize resources across this city, New York City, Boston, and other sites to ensure effective protection and response.

explicit_text: New York City was a central focus during the 9/11 attacks, especially with the World Trade Center being the target. I personally witnessed the attacks while in North Carolina and knew our teams would be heavily involved in the response there.
